title: Cat Dark Brown or Cola-Colored Urine: Causes and Emergency Triage
meta_desc: If your cat has dark brown or cola-colored urine, it is a medical emergency. Learn why this happens and why you must see a veterinarian immediately.
content_html:
What Does Dark Brown or Cola-Colored Urine Mean?

Finding dark brown or cola-colored urine in your cat's litter box is a serious symptom. In veterinary medicine, this discoloration is often caused by the presence of broken-down red blood cells (hemoglobinuria), muscle tissue breakdown (myoglobinuria), or concentrated old blood. It indicates that your cat's body is processing something toxic or experiencing a significant internal health crisis.

Is This an Emergency?

Yes. The urgency level for this symptom is High. Dark brown urine is never normal and often points to life-threatening conditions such as acetaminophen (Tylenol) toxicity, severe hemolytic anemia, or advanced kidney disease. If your cat is also showing signs of lethargy, labored breathing, or pale/yellowish gums, you should transport them to an emergency veterinary hospital immediately. Delaying treatment can lead to irreversible organ failure.

Why Taking a Photo Helps Triage

When you contact your vet or arrive at the clinic, providing a clear photo of the urine can be life-saving. Since cats often urinate in private, showing the veterinarian the exact color and consistency on a white paper towel or in the litter helps them differentiate between hematuria (fresh blood) and other pigments. This visual evidence allows the medical team to prioritize your cat's case and begin the correct diagnostic tests much faster.

faqs.question: Can dehydration cause brown urine in cats?
faqs.answer: While severe dehydration can make urine very dark yellow or orange, true brown or cola-colored urine usually indicates the breakdown of red blood cells or muscle tissue, which requires immediate medical intervention.

faqs.question: What toxins cause brown urine in cats?
faqs.answer: Common toxins include acetaminophen (Tylenol), onions, garlic, and certain zinc-containing objects. These substances cause oxidative damage to red blood cells, turning the urine a dark, muddy brown color.

faqs.question: What should I do if my cat is acting normally but has brown urine?
faqs.answer: Even if your cat is acting fine, dark brown urine is an internal red flag. Some conditions, like certain types of poisoning, may cause internal damage before your cat shows outward signs of collapse. You should still seek veterinary care immediately.
